text: What are emergent properties?
choices: Properties found only in individual atoms and never in larger structures
New properties that result from the arrangement and interaction of parts in a more complex structure
Properties that disappear when molecules bond together
Properties found only in lipids
explain: Emergent properties arise from interactions among parts and appear at higher levels of organization.

text: Why is carbon considered the backbone of many biological molecules?
choices: Carbon can form four covalent bonds, allowing many stable structures
Carbon has no valence electrons, so it does not react
Carbon bonds only to itself
Carbon is the only element found in living things
explain: Carbon's four valence electrons let it form diverse, stable structures (chains, rings, branches).

text: Which elements make up the majority of living matter?
choices: Sodium, calcium, iron, and chlorine
Carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and nitrogen
Gold, copper, zinc, and magnesium
Helium, neon, argon, and krypton
explain: C, H, O, and N account for most of the mass of biological molecules.

text: What is an isomer?
choices: A molecule with the same molecular formula as another molecule but a different structure
A molecule with a different molecular formula but the same structure
A special type of protein
A bond that holds nucleotides together
explain: Isomers have the same formula but different arrangements of atoms, leading to different properties.

text: What type of bond joins nucleotides together in a nucleic acid strand?
choices: Peptide bond
Glycosidic bond
Phosphodiester bond
Ionic bond
explain: Phosphodiester bonds link the sugar-phosphate backbone of nucleic acid strands.

text: Which level of protein structure involves interactions between multiple polypeptide chains?
choices: Primary structure
Secondary structure
Tertiary structure
Quaternary structure
explain: Quaternary structure describes how multiple polypeptide subunits assemble into a functional protein complex.

text: What is denaturation?
choices: The formation of a new peptide bond
The loss of a macromolecule’s normal shape, often causing loss of function
The replication of DNA
The breakdown of lipids into glycerol and fatty acids
explain: Denaturation disrupts the normal folding of macromolecules, commonly proteins, impairing function.

text: Which statement best describes a saturated fatty acid?
choices: It contains one or more double bonds in its hydrocarbon chain
It contains only single bonds in its hydrocarbon chain
It contains a phosphate group instead of a carboxyl group
It is always part of a nucleic acid
explain: Saturated fatty acids have only single carbon-carbon bonds, allowing tight packing.

text: Why are proteins considered highly diverse molecules?
choices: All proteins are made from different types of monomers in every organism
The sequence and properties of amino acids can vary greatly, producing many shapes and functions
Proteins never fold into three-dimensional structures
Proteins are made only of carbon and hydrogen
explain: Variations in amino acid sequences and side-chain properties produce wide structural and functional diversity.

text: Two molecules have the same chemical formula, but one is used for quick energy in cells while the other contributes to structural support in a cell wall. Which statement best explains this difference?
choices: Molecules with the same formula must have the same function
Their atoms may be arranged differently, causing different shapes and biological roles
One molecule must contain no carbon
Biological function depends only on mass, not structure
explain: Different arrangements (isomers or different linkage patterns) can result in very different biological properties and functions.

text: A researcher compares two proteins with identical primary structure. Protein A functions normally in the cytoplasm, but Protein B has been exposed to high temperature and no longer functions. Which conclusion is most supported?
choices: Protein B’s amino acid sequence was destroyed by heat
Protein B likely lost secondary or tertiary structure that is necessary for function
Protein B must have turned into a lipid
Protein A is not actually a protein because it still works
explain: Heat can disrupt secondary and tertiary interactions, causing loss of functional folding despite an unchanged amino acid sequence.

text: A DNA molecule contains an unusually high percentage of guanine and cytosine bases. What is the best prediction?
choices: The DNA may require more energy to separate because G-C pairs form more hydrogen bonds
The DNA will have fewer total bonds because G-C pairs are weak
The DNA cannot store genetic information
The DNA is actually a protein because it has many bonds
explain: G-C pairs form three hydrogen bonds, increasing the stability and energy required to separate the strands.

text: A polypeptide contains many nonpolar amino acids clustered together in one region. In a watery environment, what is the most likely result?
choices: That region will tend to fold toward the inside of the protein
That region will strongly attract water and remain exposed
The polypeptide will automatically become RNA
The peptide bonds in that region will be replaced by hydrogen bonds
explain: Nonpolar side chains minimize contact with water and often become buried in the protein interior during folding.

text: A student says, "Because water is polar, it can dissolve every substance found in cells." Which response is best?
choices: The statement is correct because polarity allows water to dissolve anything
The statement is incorrect because nonpolar molecules often do not dissolve well in water
The statement is incorrect because water is actually nonpolar
The statement is incorrect because only proteins dissolve in water
explain: Water dissolves many polar and ionic substances but often cannot dissolve nonpolar molecules like many lipids.

text: A mutation changes a codon in a gene so that one acidic amino acid in a protein is replaced by a basic amino acid. Why could this alter protein structure?
choices: The change could alter ionic interactions among side chains, affecting folding
The change always increases the number of nucleotides in the protein
The change removes all hydrogen bonds from the protein
The change means the protein no longer has a primary structure
explain: Replacing an acidic with a basic residue can change charge interactions and disrupt folding and function.

text: Which statement best explains why phospholipids are useful in membrane formation?
choices: They are entirely nonpolar and repel both water and lipids
Their hydrophilic and hydrophobic regions allow them to self-organize in aqueous environments
They are polymers made of repeating nucleotide monomers
Their peptide bonds create rigid membrane walls
explain: Amphipathic phospholipids self-assemble so hydrophobic tails are sequestered away from water and hydrophilic heads face the aqueous environment.

text: A scientist places an enzyme in a solution far outside its normal pH range. The enzyme remains intact as a chain of amino acids but loses activity. Which interpretation is best?
choices: The enzyme’s primary structure was hydrolyzed completely
The enzyme’s shape was altered enough to affect substrate binding
The enzyme became a carbohydrate
The substrate was turned into ATP
explain: Extreme pH can change side-chain charges and disrupt folding required for substrate binding, reducing activity.

text: A student mixes glycerol with three fatty acids and forms ester linkages among them. What has the student produced, and what is its likely biological role?
choices: A triglyceride used mainly for long-term energy storage
A phospholipid used mainly to store genetic information
A protein used for catalysis
A polysaccharide used for structural support
explain: Glycerol plus three fatty acids forms a triglyceride, an efficient long-term energy store.

text: Students investigate how buffers affect pH stability. They prepare two beakers: Beaker 1: distilled water; Beaker 2: a buffered solution. They add the same small amount of acid to each beaker and measure pH change. Results: Beaker 1: pH drops from 7.0 to 4.9; Beaker 2: pH drops from 7.0 to 6.8. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The buffered solution resists changes in pH better than distilled water
Distilled water is a stronger buffer because its pH changed more
The buffered solution contains no hydrogen ions
Distilled water must contain more lipids than the buffered solution
explain: The smaller pH change in the buffered solution shows it resists pH change compared to distilled water.
experiment: Two beakers (distilled water and buffered solution) received equal acid; pH changes were recorded showing greater stability in the buffered solution.

text: A researcher compares two proteins that differ by one amino acid. Protein 1 binds its substrate normally. Protein 2 does not bind the substrate, even though both proteins are the same length. Which hypothesis is most reasonable?
choices: The amino acid substitution likely altered the shape or chemistry of the binding site
Protein 2 must have lost every peptide bond
Protein 1 is actually DNA because it can bind a target
Protein 2 must have gained many extra nucleotides
explain: A single substitution can change the binding site's shape or chemistry, reducing substrate binding.
experiment: Binding assays comparing wild-type and mutant proteins showed loss of binding in the mutant despite identical length.

text: Students test the ability of different substances to dissolve in water. Substance X dissolves readily. Substance Y forms a separate layer. Which conclusion is most supported?
choices: Substance Y is likely more polar than Substance X
Substance X is likely polar or charged, while Substance Y is likely more nonpolar
Substance Y must be a nucleic acid
Substance X must be a lipid because it dissolved
explain: Polar or charged substances dissolve in water; nonpolar substances tend to separate into layers.
experiment: Equal volumes of two substances were added to water; one dissolved while the other formed a layer, indicating differing polarity.

text: A teacher gives students two unknown biological molecules. Molecule A: contains C,H,O; repeating monosaccharides; used for energy storage. Molecule B: contains C,H,O,N; made of amino acid monomers; can function as an enzyme. Which identification is most likely?
choices: Molecule A is a carbohydrate and Molecule B is a protein
Molecule A is a nucleic acid and Molecule B is a lipid
Molecule A is a protein and Molecule B is a carbohydrate
Molecule A is a phospholipid and Molecule B is DNA
explain: The descriptions match a carbohydrate (energy storage polysaccharide) and a protein (enzyme).
experiment: Elemental and monomer analysis matched the given descriptions, indicating carbohydrate and protein identities.

text: A lab group examines the effect of temperature on enzyme activity. As temperature rises from 10°C to 37°C, the reaction rate increases. At 60°C, the rate drops sharply. Which explanation best fits the data?
choices: Higher temperatures always destroy substrate molecules
The enzyme likely works faster up to an optimal temperature, then loses shape at very high temperatures
The enzyme becomes a lipid at 60°C
The reaction stops at 60°C because water is no longer polar
explain: Enzymes have optimal temperatures; higher temperatures can denature enzymes, reducing activity.
experiment: Reaction rates were measured across temperatures showing an optimum near physiological temperature and denaturation at high temperatures.

text: A researcher compares two nucleic acid strands. Strand X contains deoxyribose and thymine. Strand Y contains ribose and uracil. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Strand X is RNA and Strand Y is DNA
Strand X is DNA and Strand Y is RNA
Both strands are proteins because they contain nitrogen
Both strands must be lipids because they contain carbon and hydrogen
explain: Deoxyribose + thymine indicates DNA; ribose + uracil indicates RNA.
experiment: Sugar and base analysis identified deoxyribose/thymine vs ribose/uracil, supporting DNA vs RNA assignment.

text: Students compare a saturated fat and an unsaturated fat at room temperature. The saturated fat is solid, while the unsaturated fat is liquid. Which hypothesis best explains the difference?
choices: Saturated fats contain double bonds that increase fluidity
Unsaturated fats have bends in their tails that reduce tight packing
Saturated fats contain more amino acids, making them rigid
Unsaturated fats are more basic, so they melt more easily
explain: Double bonds introduce kinks that prevent tight packing, increasing fluidity of unsaturated fats.
experiment: Phase behavior of two fats was compared at room temperature; the unsaturated-rich sample remained liquid.

text: A mutation changes one nucleotide in a gene, causing one amino acid in the resulting enzyme to change. The enzyme still folds, but it works much more slowly. Which conclusion is most reasonable?
choices: Even a small change in primary structure can affect enzyme function
The mutation proves that amino acid sequence does not matter
The mutation must have changed the enzyme into RNA
The enzyme must now contain no peptide bonds
explain: Single amino acid substitutions can alter catalytic efficiency by affecting active site geometry or dynamics.
experiment: Kinetic assays showed reduced turnover number for the mutant enzyme compared to wild type.

text: A scientist studies a protein found in the cytoplasm and notices that many polar and charged amino acids are exposed on the outer surface. Which explanation best fits this observation?
choices: Cytoplasmic proteins often expose hydrophilic residues because they interact with a watery environment
Cytoplasmic proteins must always be made of nucleotides
The outer surface of all proteins is always nonpolar
Charged amino acids can exist only in the center of a protein
explain: Proteins in aqueous environments often present polar/charged residues on their surfaces to interact with solvent and other molecules.
experiment: Surface residue analysis and solvent-accessibility mapping showed enrichment of polar residues on the protein's exterior.

text: Students are given three statements about a newly discovered biomolecule: 1) It is made from monomers. 2) Its function depends heavily on three-dimensional shape. 3) Changing one monomer can alter function. Which macromolecule is most consistent with all three statements?
choices: Protein
Water
Sodium chloride
Oxygen gas
explain: Proteins are polymers of amino acids whose function depends on 3D structure and can be altered by single substitutions.
experiment: Functional assays and monomer analysis supported that the molecule behaved like a protein sensitive to single-monomer changes.
